summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the value of learning from the mistakes of more talented people. He highlights two sources of wisdom: Rick Cattell's engineering career advice emphasizing that good technology alone isn't enough for success, and Hugh Macleod's creative advice about self-reliance and persistence. The post serves as a brief curation of hard-won lessons from experienced practitioners.
key_points: Even highly talented people make mistakes, which should be reassuring to all developers
Good technology accounts for only about 10% of real-world success
Building things that go unused is a common and frustrating career pitfall
Creative and business success requires self-reliance rather than waiting to be discovered
Putting in the hours and taking responsibility for your own experience are essential
money_quotes: It's always reassuring to be reminded that people much more talented than myself make mistakes, too.
Good technology is only 10% of 'real world' success
It's frustrating to spend years of your career building things that aren't used
The idea doesn't have to be big. It just has to change the world.
If your biz plan depends on you suddenly being 'discovered' by some big shot, your plan will probably fail.
You are responsible for your own experience.
key_insight: Learning from the publicly shared mistakes of talented people is one of the most valuable shortcuts to avoiding wasted years building things that don't matter.
